title: M1 Part 1 Data Analysis Exercise
text:
Load Packages and Dataset
For this assignment we must download the dslabs package containing the gapminder dataset. Loading the package and dataset will ensure that all functions and data are correctly available.

# install.packages("dslabs") # run once if not installed
library(dslabs)

This is to create subsets for analysis within our already designated subset of Africa. We have two specific relationships of interest.

Life expectancy vs. infant mortality
Life expectancy vs. population




Plot Life Expectancy vs Infant Mortality

ggplot(africadata, aes(x = infant_mortality, y = life_expectancy)) +
  geom_point() +   # scatter plot points
  labs(title = "Life Expectancy vs Infant Mortality (Africa)",
       x = "Infant Mortality",
       y = "Life Expectancy") +
  theme_minimal()  # clean visual style

Warning: Removed 226 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).









This allows us to generate a scatterplot to show the relationship between life expectancy and infant morality in African countries.



Plot Life Expectancy vs Population

ggplot(africadata, aes(x = population, y = life_expectancy)) +
  geom_point() +               # scatter plot points
  scale_x_log10() +            # log-scale for population
  labs(title = "Life Expectancy vs Population (Africa, log scale)",
       x = "Population",
       y = "Life Expectancy") +
  theme_minimal()              # clean visual style

Warning: Removed 51 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).









Now we can examine the relaionshio between population size and life expectancy. However, we log-scale the x-axia, ‘x’, due to population variability. This scaling will improve the readability of our graph visual.



Focus on the Year 2000

year2000 <- africadata[africadata$year == 2000, ]

Some data years have inconsistent data or missing data. Therefore, we will focus on the year 2000 for a cleaner read.



Plots for Year 2000

Life Expectancy vs Infant Mortality

plot(year2000$infant_mortality, year2000$life_expectancy,
     xlab="Infant Mortality",
     ylab="Life Expectancy",
     main="Year 2000: Life Expectancy vs Infant Mortality",
     pch=16)










Life Expectancy vs Population

plot(year2000$population, year2000$life_expectancy,
     xlab="Population (log scale)",
     ylab="Life Expectancy",
     main="Year 2000: Life Expectancy vs Population",
     log="x", pch=16)












Linear Models
We will use simple linear models to see if life expectancy can be predicted from infant mortality or population size in the year 2000.

# Life Expectancy ~ Infant Mortality
fit1 <- lm(life_expectancy ~ infant_mortality, data=year2000)
summary(fit1)


Call:
lm(formula = life_expectancy ~ infant_mortality, data = year2000)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-22.6651  -3.7087   0.9914   4.0408   8.6817 

Coefficients:
                 Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)      71.29331    2.42611  29.386  < 2e-16 ***
infant_mortality -0.18916    0.02869  -6.594 2.83e-08 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 6.221 on 49 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.4701,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.4593 
F-statistic: 43.48 on 1 and 49 DF,  p-value: 2.826e-08

# Life Expectancy ~ Population
fit2 <- lm(life_expectancy ~ population, data=year2000)
summary(fit2)

lm(formula = life_expectancy ~ population, data = year2000)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max

Life_expectancy ~ infant_mortality tells us the relationship between life expectancy and infant mortality
life_expectancy ~ population will show the relationship between country size and life expectancy
summary ( ) will provide our descriptive statistics

It looks like this dataset only has three variables: year, gas, and concentrations. Specifically, this dataset contains concentration values of three greenhouse gases (carbon dioxide CO2, methane CH4 and nitrous oxide N2O) from year 20 to 2000.

anyNA(greenhouse_gases)

[1] FALSE


It also looks like there aren’t any missing values in this dataset.
Let’s take a quick look at how these gases change over time.

ggplot(greenhouse_gases, aes(x=year, y=concentration, group=gas))+
     geom_line(aes(color=gas))+
     theme_bw()








Gas concentrations seemed to have shot up at some point past 1500. Using historical context, let’s take a look at the numbers only from 1760-2000 (from the beginning of the industrial revolution).

#Subset the data from 1500-2000
greenhouse_gases1 <- greenhouse_gases[greenhouse_gases$year >= 1760 & greenhouse_gases$year <= 2000,]

#Check the subset
str(greenhouse_gases1)

'data.frame':   39 obs. of  3 variables:
 $ year         : num  1760 1780 1800 1820 1840 1860 1880 1900 1920 1940 ...
 $ gas          : chr  "CO2" "CO2" "CO2" "CO2" ...
 $ concentration: num  276 278 283 283 284 ...

summary(greenhouse_gases1)

 Median :1880   Mode  :character   Median : 302.6  
 Mean   :1880                      Mean   : 516.2  
 3rd Qu.:1940                      3rd Qu.: 733.0  
 Max.   :2000                      Max.   :1703.4  


The 1500-2000 subset now has 39 observations as opposed to the original dataset with 300. Let’s create the same graph as before, but now using this new subset.

ggplot(greenhouse_gases1, aes(x=year, y=concentration, group=gas))+
     geom_line(aes(color=gas))+
     theme_bw()








Focusing on methane, let’s create a simple linear model to determine whether how its concentration was compared with time.

#Filter CH4 
greenhouse_gases2 <- greenhouse_gases1[greenhouse_gases1$gas == "CH4",]

#Use lm function to fit CH4 concentration as the outcome and year as the predictor
fit3 <- lm(concentration ~ year, data=greenhouse_gases2)
summary(fit3)


Call:
lm(formula = concentration ~ year, data = greenhouse_gases2)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-173.37 -121.21  -51.33  116.74  300.93 

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) -5879.402   1089.379  -5.397 0.000218 ***
year            3.641      0.579   6.288 5.93e-05 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 156.2 on 11 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.7824,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.7626 
F-statistic: 39.54 on 1 and 11 DF,  p-value: 5.933e-05


CH4 concentration is strongly positively associated with year (3.641, p=5.93e-05). For each year that went by, CH4 concentration increased by 3.6 units.

title: Dataset Description
text:
The dataset I selected is “Lyme disease public use line-listed data without geographic identifiers (2022–2023)” from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC). It is available through the CDC’s open data portal (Data.CDC.gov) . This dataset contains de-identified, individual-level (line-listed) records of reported Lyme disease cases in the United States for years 2022–2023. Each row represents a single reported case. The dataset includes demographic and clinical variables such as age group, sex, race/ethnicity, and illness onset information. Geographic identifiers (such as state or county) have been removed to protect privacy.


https://data.cdc.gov/National-Center-for-Emerging-and-Zoonotic-Infectio/Lyme-disease-public-use-line-listed-data-without-g/9mtj-y2ba/about_data

title: Dataset Description
text:
Variable “Year”
Our data currently includes years 2022 and 2023, and we constructed a bar graph to visualize the number of cases in each year. In 2022, there were 62,549 cases while there were 89,469 cases in 2023. Collectively, there were 152,018 cases of Lyme disease.

ggplot(lyme, aes(x = factor(Year))) +
  # Bar plot of Cases #
  geom_bar(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Lyme Disease Cases by Year",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()







# percentage #
lyme %>%
  count(Year) %>%
  mutate(percent = round(n / sum(n) * 100, 1))

# A tibble: 2 × 3

  <dbl> <int>   <dbl>
1  2022 62549    41.1
2  2023 89469    58.9

title: About me
text:
My name is Talia C. Swanson, and I am currently a second-year Master of Public Health (MPH) student in the Epidemiology concentration. I completed my Bachelor of Science in Biology at the University of Georgia this past Summer and am originally from Atlanta, Georgia.
My interest in public health grew through my work with IMPACT UGA Alternative Service Breaks, a program that sends students to cities across the Southeast to explore socioeconomic challenges and contribute to initiatives supporting these communities. I participated in trips to Asheboro, NC, focusing on affordable housing, and Charlotte, NC, focusing on education access.

title: About me
text:
Research Interests & Goals
My research interests focus on environmental exposures, specifically particulate matter, PM2.5, and how these exposures contribute to the development of chronic disease. I am also interested in social determinants of health, with an emphasis on how cultural choices and structures influence health outcomes. Currently, I am supporting a Household Air Pollution Intervention Network, (HAPIN), follow-up study examining the effects of PM2.5 exposure from traditional cooking methods on the human microbiome and its relationship to respiratory health.
In addition to my epidemiologic interests, I currently support a project in the ACCESS Lab within the Cellular Biology department that examines undergraduate experiences in STEM, with a focus on how institutional structures and learning environments shape student engagement.
Long term, I hope to study environmental exposures and how they contribute to respiratory and nuerodegenerative disease while pursuing a career in the federal sector.

title: About me
text:
Data Analysis Experience
I have taken EPID 7010 and BIOS 7010/7020 that focused on using SAS and Stata to produce survivial models, regression visuals, ANOVA, and T-tests for statistical analysis. Additionally, I have worked on an independent project in my EPID 7650 class assessing major depressive episodes among adolescents aged 12–17, using multivariate regression modeling in R to examine the relationship between the occurrence of major depressive episodes and racial and cultural demographic factors.

title: About me
text:
Hobbies
Outside of academics, I really enjoy musical instruments, including the flute, piccolo, and violin. I also enjoy reading in my spare time, particularly fantasy and myth-based genres involving vampires, elves, warlocks, and related themes. I enjoy exploring new foods and recently Pad Thai for the very first time during my study abroad trip to Bangkok!

To adjust the spacing between categories (bars) in my Halloween candy graph, I asked an ChatGPT: “How do I increase or decrease the spacing between categories in a horizontal bar chart in ggplot2?” AI suggested changing the width parameter in geom_col() and explained that lower width values create more space between bars. This table is show the average win sahre based on candy type and categories based on the ranking





library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

candy_summary <- tibble(
  candy_type = c("Chocolate","Fruit","Peanuts & nuts","Crispy", "Caramel","Nougat","Multi-piece","Candy bar","Hard candy"),
  avg_win = c(61,44,57,64,60,66,41,61,47),
  value_add = c(19.9,10.3,10.1,9.0,3.4,2.4,-0.2,-0.7,-4.9)
)

# positions for rows
candy_summary <- candy_summary %>%
  mutate(row = rev(row_number()))

ggplot(candy_summary) +

  # shaded value column
  geom_rect(aes(
    xmin = 2.6,
    xmax = 4.6,
    ymin = row - .45,
    ymax = row + .45,
    fill = value_add
  ),
  color = NA) +

  # candy type column
  geom_text(aes(x = 0, y = row, label = candy_type),
    hjust = 0,
    family = "serif",
    size = 6) +

  # avg win share column
  geom_text(aes(x = 2.2, y = row,
    label = ifelse(row == max(row),
      paste0(avg_win, "%"),
      avg_win)),
            hjust = 1,
            family = "serif",
            size = 6) +

  # value add numbers
  geom_text(aes(x = 2.7, y = row,
    label = sprintf("%+.1f", value_add)),
      hjust = 0,
      family = "serif",
      size = 6) +

  # teal to pink gradient
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "#D9B0BC",
    mid = "#F0F0F0",
    high = "#4DA8A9",
    midpoint = 0
  ) +

  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-0.1, 4.8)) +

  labs(title = NULL) +

  theme_void() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA)
  ) +

  # headers in the graph
  annotate("text", x = 0, y = max(candy_summary$row) + 1,
    label = "CANDY TYPE",
    family = "serif", fontface = "bold", size = 7, hjust = 0) +
  annotate("text", x = 2.2, y = max(candy_summary$row) + 1,
    label = "AVG. WIN SHARE",
    family = "serif", fontface = "bold", size = 7, hjust = 1) +
  annotate("text", x = 3.6, y = max(candy_summary$row) + 1,
    label = "VALUE ADD TO WIN %",
    family = "serif", fontface = "bold", size = 7, hjust = 0)











Moving forward, I wanted to a different create publication ready table. For my dataset I presented the top 10 halloween candies based on the dataset. This is not a reproduced table, instead, I decided to make a table to display the top 10 candies by winning percentages. It is taking the first 10 groups from the long original chart and placing them within a table. For this, I asked the AI to generate a table showing the top 10 Halloween candies based on win percentage from a dataset of head-to-head candy matchups. I wanted the table to be ranked and have color-coded performance. The AI produced R code using the gt package with formatted numbers, colored cells, a header, and footnotes for clarity.

library(dplyr)
library(gt)

Warning: package 'gt' was built under R version 4.5.2

library(scales)


Attaching package: 'scales'


The following object is masked from 'package:purrr':

    discard


The following object is masked from 'package:readr':

    col_factor

# Top 10 candies by win percentage
top10 <- candy %>%
  arrange(desc(winpercent)) %>%
  slice(1:10) %>%
  mutate(
    Rank = row_number()
  ) %>%
  select(Rank, competitorname, winpercent)

# Calculate automatic summary statistic
mean_top10 <- mean(top10$winpercent)

# Create styled table
top10 %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(
    title = md("**Top 10 Halloween Candies by Win Percentage**"),
    subtitle = "Ranked based on head-to-head matchup performance"
  ) %>%
  fmt_number(
    columns = winpercent,
    decimals = 1
  ) %>%
  data_color(
    columns = winpercent,
    colors = col_numeric(
      palette = c("#fde0dd", "#31a354"),
      domain = NULL
    )
  ) %>%
  cols_label(
    competitorname = "Candy",
    winpercent = "Win Percentage"
  ) %>%
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = md(
      paste0(
        "*Mean win percentage (Top 10):* **",
        round(mean_top10, 1),
        "%**"
      )
    )
  ) %>%
  tab_footnote(
    footnote = "Win percentage calculated from 85,000+ head-to-head candy matchups.",
    locations = cells_column_labels(columns = winpercent)
  )

title: Presentation Exercise – Halloween Candy
text:
Moving forward, I wanted to a different create publication ready table. For my dataset I presented the top 10 halloween candies based on the dataset. This is not a reproduced table, instead, I decided to make a table to display the top 10 candies by winning percentages. It is taking the first 10 groups from the long original chart and placing them within a table. For this, I asked the AI to generate a table showing the top 10 Halloween candies based on win percentage from a dataset of head-to-head candy matchups. I wanted the table to be ranked and have color-coded performance. The AI produced R code using the gt package with formatted numbers, colored cells, a header, and footnotes for clarity.

library(dplyr)
library(gt)

Warning: package 'gt' was built under R version 4.5.2

library(scales)


Attaching package: 'scales'


The following object is masked from 'package:purrr':

    discard


The following object is masked from 'package:readr':

    col_factor

# Top 10 candies by win percentage
top10 <- candy %>%
  arrange(desc(winpercent)) %>%
  slice(1:10) %>%
  mutate(
    Rank = row_number()
  ) %>%
  select(Rank, competitorname, winpercent)

# Calculate automatic summary statistic
mean_top10 <- mean(top10$winpercent)

# Create styled table
top10 %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(
    title = md("**Top 10 Halloween Candies by Win Percentage**"),
    subtitle = "Ranked based on head-to-head matchup performance"
  ) %>%
  fmt_number(
    columns = winpercent,
    decimals = 1
  ) %>%
  data_color(
    columns = winpercent,
    colors = col_numeric(
      palette = c("#fde0dd", "#31a354"),
      domain = NULL
    )
  ) %>%
  cols_label(
    competitorname = "Candy",
    winpercent = "Win Percentage"
  ) %>%
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = md(
      paste0(
        "*Mean win percentage (Top 10):* **",
        round(mean_top10, 1),
        "%**"
      )
    )
  ) %>%
  tab_footnote(
    footnote = "Win percentage calculated from 85,000+ head-to-head candy matchups.",
    locations = cells_column_labels(columns = winpercent)
  )

library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

candy_summary <- tibble(
  candy_type = c("Chocolate","Fruit","Peanuts & nuts","Crispy", "Caramel","Nougat","Multi-piece","Candy bar","Hard candy"),
  avg_win = c(61,44,57,64,60,66,41,61,47),
  value_add = c(19.9,10.3,10.1,9.0,3.4,2.4,-0.2,-0.7,-4.9)
)

# positions for rows
candy_summary <- candy_summary %>%
  mutate(row = rev(row_number()))

ggplot(candy_summary) +

  # shaded value column
  geom_rect(aes(
    xmin = 2.6,
    xmax = 4.6,
    ymin = row - .45,
    ymax = row + .45,
    fill = value_add
  ),
  color = NA) +

  # candy type column
  geom_text(aes(x = 0, y = row, label = candy_type),
    hjust = 0,
    family = "serif",
    size = 6) +

  # avg win share column
  geom_text(aes(x = 2.2, y = row,
    label = ifelse(row == max(row),
      paste0(avg_win, "%"),
      avg_win)),
            hjust = 1,
            family = "serif",
            size = 6) +

  # value add numbers
  geom_text(aes(x = 2.7, y = row,
    label = sprintf("%+.1f", value_add)),
      hjust = 0,
      family = "serif",
      size = 6) +

  # teal to pink gradient
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "#D9B0BC",
    mid = "#F0F0F0",
    high = "#4DA8A9",
    midpoint = 0
  ) +

  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-0.1, 4.8)) +

  labs(title = NULL) +

  theme_void() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA)
  ) +

  # headers in the graph
  annotate("text", x = 0, y = max(candy_summary$row) + 1,
    label = "CANDY TYPE",
    family = "serif", fontface = "bold", size = 7, hjust = 0) +
  annotate("text", x = 2.2, y = max(candy_summary$row) + 1,
    label = "AVG. WIN SHARE",
    family = "serif", fontface = "bold", size = 7, hjust = 1) +
  annotate("text", x = 3.6, y = max(candy_summary$row) + 1,
    label = "VALUE ADD TO WIN %",
    family = "serif", fontface = "bold", size = 7, hjust = 0)

The dataset contains 2,678 observations and 17 variables. Each row represents a measurement at a specific time point for a participant. Key variables include ID for participant identification, DV for measured drug concentration, TIME for when the measurement occurred, DOSE for administered dose, and OCC for dosing occasion. Participant characteristics such as AGE, SEX, RACE, WT (weight), and HT (height) are also included.




ggplot(drug_data, aes(x = TIME, y = DV, group = ID, color = factor(DOSE))) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.3) +
  labs(
    title = "Drug Concentration Over Time",
    x = "Time",
    y = "Drug Concentration (DV)",
    color = "Dose"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()










Here we separate each dosing into a separate graph for easier readability with the facet_wrap function.

ggplot(drug_data, aes(x = TIME, y = DV, group = ID, color = factor(DOSE))) +
  facet_wrap(~ DOSE) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.3) +
  labs(
    title = "Drug Concentration Over Time",
    x = "Time",
    y = "Drug Concentration (DV)",
    color = "Dose"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()








This plot shows drug concentration (DV) over time for each participant. Each line represents one individual, and colors indicate different dose levels. Overall, higher doses appear to produce higher drug concentrations over time.




The dataset contains an indicator variable OCC representing dosing occasions. Some individuals received the drug more than once (OCC = 1 and OCC = 2). Since the difference between occasions is unclear and we want to avoid complications from repeated dosing, I retain only observations where OCC = 1. This ensures that the data from the participants, is from only one dosing occasion.

drug_occ1 <- drug_data %>%
  filter(OCC == 1)

glimpse(drug_occ1)

To summarize drug exposure for each participant, total drug concentration across was calcualtes across measured time points. This value is labeled as Y, and represents drug concentration for each individual.

# Keep the dosing rows, (TIME = 0)
dose_rows <- drug_occ1 %>%
  filter(TIME == 0)

title: Model Fitting Exercise
text:
Linear Model with Y - DOSE

# Linear regression with Y as outcome and DOSE as predictor
lm_fit <- lm(Y ~ DOSE, data = analysis_data)
summary(lm_fit)  # shows coefficients and R-squared


Call:
lm(formula = Y ~ DOSE, data = analysis_data)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-1284.91  -441.14   -97.22   325.84  2372.87 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  323.062    199.049   1.623    0.107    
DOSE          58.213      5.194  11.208   <2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 672.1 on 118 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.5156,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.5115 
F-statistic: 125.6 on 1 and 118 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16


This linear regression examines the relationship between dose (DOSE) and total drug exposure (Y). The estimated coefficient for dose is 58.2, meaning that for each additional unit increase in dose, total drug exposure increases by approximately 58 units on average. The model explains roughly 52% of the variation in exposure (R² = 0.516), and the F-test for the overall model is highly significant (F = 125.6, p < 0.001), indicating that dose is a meaningful predictor of total drug exposure in this dataset. The residuals suggest moderate variability around the fitted values, so while dose is important, other factors may also contribute to differences in exposure.

rmse_lm_all

[1] 590.8535

The model using only DOSE has an RMSE of 666.5 and explains about 51.6% of the variation in total drug exposure (R² = 0.516). When all predictors are included, the RMSE decreases to 590.9 and the R² increases to 0.619. This indicates that adding AGE, SEX, RACE, WT, and HT improves the model’s ability to predict drug exposure. Overall, the full model provides a better fit than the dose-only model.

title: Model Fitting Exercise
text:
Logistic Model with SEX - DOSE

# Logistic regression with SEX as outcome and DOSE as predictor
log_fit <- glm(SEX ~ DOSE, data = analysis_data, family = binomial)
summary(log_fit)


Call:
glm(formula = SEX ~ DOSE, family = binomial, data = analysis_data)

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)
(Intercept) -0.76482    0.85395  -0.896    0.370
DOSE        -0.03175    0.02432  -1.306    0.192

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 94.242  on 119  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 92.431  on 118  degrees of freedom
AIC: 96.431

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 5


This logistic regression model looks at whether drug dose predicts participant sex. The coefficient for dose is not statistically significant (p = 0.192), indicating that dose is not associated with sex in this dataset. Additionally, the small change between the null deviance and residual deviance suggests that dose does not substantially improve the model’s ability to predict sex. However, this can be expected as drug dose is not related to participant sex.

# Predicted probabilities
pred_prob <- predict(log_fit, analysis_data, type = "response")

# Convert to predicted classes
pred_class <- ifelse(pred_prob > 0.5, 2, 1)
pred_class <- factor(pred_class, levels = c(1, 2))
sex_factor <- factor(analysis_data$SEX, levels = c(1, 2))

# Accuracy
accuracy_log <- yardstick::accuracy_vec(
  truth = sex_factor,
  estimate = pred_class
)

accuracy_log

[1] 0.8666667

# ROC-AUC
roc_auc_log <- yardstick::roc_auc_vec(
  truth = sex_factor,
  estimate = pred_prob
)
roc_auc_log

[1] 0.4080529


ROC-AUC evaluates how well the model distinguishes between the two categories of sex. A value close to 0.5 suggests that the model performs little better than random guessing.

library(yardstick)

# Predicted probabilities for full logistic model
pred_prob_all <- predict(log_all, analysis_data, type = "response")

# Convert to predicted classes using 0.5 cutoff
pred_class_all <- ifelse(pred_prob_all > 0.5, 2, 1)
pred_class_all <- factor(pred_class_all, levels = c(1, 2))

# Convert true SEX to factor
sex_factor <- factor(analysis_data$SEX, levels = c(1, 2))

# Accuracy
accuracy_log_all <- yardstick::accuracy_vec(
  truth = sex_factor,
  estimate = pred_class_all
)
accuracy_log_all

[1] 0.9416667

# ROC AUC
roc_auc_log_all <- yardstick::roc_auc_vec(
  truth = sex_factor,
  estimate = pred_prob_all
)
roc_auc_log_all

[1] 0.02043269


The full logistic model predicts sex with high accuracy (94.2%), meaning most participants are classified correctly. However, the ROC-AUC is very low (0.02), indicating the model performs poorly at distinguishing between males and females. This mismatch occurs because the dataset is heavily imbalanced (many more males than females). Essentially, while the model “guesses” correctly most of the time, it does not truly capture differences between the sexes.

BMI was created combining height and weight, which may better capture body composition than heght or weight alone.

rec <- recipe(Y ~ ., data = data) %>%

The linear model provides a baseline for comparison. It assumes a linear relationship between predictors and the outcome. The RMSE from this model is approximately 581, providing a reference point for evaluating more flexible models.

lasso_mod <- linear_reg(penalty = 0.1, mixture = 1) %>%

The LASSO regression produces an RMSE of approximately 582, which very similar RMSE to the linear model. This is expected because the penalty parameter is small, meaning that the model behaves similarly to a standard linear regression.

title: Machine Learning Models Exercise
text:
LASSO tuning (no cv)

# Recipe (SEX)
model_recipe <- recipe(Y ~ ., data = data) %>%
  step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors(), one_hot = TRUE) %>%
  step_zv(all_predictors())

# LASSO model (tunable)
lasso_spec <- linear_reg(penalty = tune(), mixture = 1) %>%
  set_engine("glmnet")

# LASSO workflow
lasso_workflow <- workflow() %>%
  add_model(lasso_spec) %>%
  add_recipe(model_recipe)

title: Machine Learning Models Exercise
text:
Defining your penalty grid

penalty_grid <- tibble(
  penalty = 10^seq(-5, 2, length.out = 50)
)


# Apparent resampling (full dataset)
resamples_app <- apparent(data)

# Tune LASSO over full dataset
lasso_tune_res <- tune_grid(
  lasso_workflow,
  resamples = resamples_app,
  grid = penalty_grid,
  metrics = metric_set(rmse)
)

As the LASSO penalty increases, the RMSE increases. This is expected because larger penalties shrink coefficients toward zero, reducing model flexibility and potentially leading to underfitting. With smaller penalties, the model behaves more like a standard linear regression.
However, this tuning is performed on the full dataset without cross-validation, meaning the model is evaluated on the same data used for training. This leads to overly optimistic performance estimates and increases the risk of overfitting.

# Random Forest model (tunable)
rf_spec <- rand_forest(
  mtry = tune(),
  min_n = tune(),
  trees = 300
) %>%
  set_engine("ranger", seed = 1234) %>%
  set_mode("regression")

# Random Forest workflow 
rf_workflow <- workflow() %>%
  add_model(rf_spec) %>%
  add_recipe(model_recipe)


# Define 5-fold CV repeated 5 times
cv_folds <- vfold_cv(data, v = 5, repeats = 5)

# Tune LASSO with CV
lasso_cv_res <- tune_grid(
  lasso_workflow,
  resamples = cv_folds,
  grid = penalty_grid,
  metrics = metric_set(rmse)
)

# Random Forest tuning with CV
rf_grid <- grid_regular(
  mtry(range = c(1, 7)),
  min_n(range = c(1, 21)),
  levels = 7
)

rf_cv_res <- tune_grid(
  rf_workflow,
  resamples = cv_folds,
  grid = rf_grid,
  metrics = metric_set(rmse)
)

autoplot(rf_cv_res)








When using cross-validation, the RMSE values are generally higher compared to tuning without cross-validation. This is because the model is evaluated on unseen data in each fold, providing a more realistic estimate of performance. Cross-validation helps prevent overfitting and gives a better indication of how the model will perform on new data.

title: Machine Learning Models Exercise
text:
Select best parameters

# Best LASSO penalty
best_lasso <- select_best(lasso_cv_res, metric = "rmse")

# Best RF parameters
best_rf <- select_best(rf_cv_res, metric = "rmse")

The best LASSO penalty minimizes RMSE across CV folds. Likewise, the best Random Forest parameters minimize RMSE and will be used to finalize each model .

title: Machine Learning Models Exercise
text:
Final models

# Final LASSO model
final_lasso <- finalize_workflow(
  lasso_workflow,
  best_lasso
) %>%
  fit(data)

# Final Random Forest model
final_rf <- finalize_workflow(
  rf_workflow,
  best_rf
) %>%
  fit(data)

Using the selected optimal parameter, we fit the final LASSO and Random Forest models to the full dataset. These models will be used for predictions and comparison.

title: Machine Learning Models Exercise

Predictions and RMSE

lasso_preds <- predict(final_lasso, data) %>% bind_cols(data)
rf_preds <- predict(final_rf, data) %>% bind_cols(data)

The RMSE values quantify model predition on the full dataset. For this dataset, the LASSO model has an RMSE of ~582, while the Random Forest model has an RMSE of ~467. This indicates that the Random Forest captures patterns in data better, likely due to its ability to model nonlinear relationships that the LASSO model cannot.

Key variables include ID for participant identification, DV for measured drug concentration, TIME for when the measurement occurred, DOSE for administered dose, and OCC for dosing occasion. Prepare Data for Modeling

title: Model Fitting Exercise
text:
Model Performance on Training Data
To evaluate model performance on the training data, predictions are generated for each model and compared with the observed values of total drug exposure (Y). The root mean squared error (RMSE) measures the average magnitude of prediction error, with lower values indicating better predictive accuracy. By comparing RMSE values across models, we can assess whether including additional predictors improves the model’s ability to explain variation in drug exposure.

pred1 <- predict(model1_fit, train_data) %>%
  bind_cols(train_data)

pred2 <- predict(model2_fit, train_data) %>%
  bind_cols(train_data)

rmse_model1 <- rmse(pred1, truth = Y, estimate = .pred)
rmse_model2 <- rmse(pred2, truth = Y, estimate = .pred)

rmse_model1

# A tibble: 1 × 3
  .metric .estimator .estimate
  <chr>   <chr>          <dbl>
1 rmse    standard        703.

rmse_model2

# A tibble: 1 × 3
  .metric .estimator .estimate
  <chr>   <chr>          <dbl>
1 rmse    standard        627.


The model using dose as the only predictor produces an RMSE of approximately 703. When additional participant characteristics (AGE, SEX, WT, and HT) are included, the RMSE decreases to about 627. This reduction in prediction error suggests that incorporating variables improves the model’s ability to predict total drug exposure compared with using dose alone.

title: Model Fitting Exercise
text:
Null Model
To provide a baseline for comparison, a null model is constructed. The null model predicts total drug exposure for every participant using only the mean value of Y from the training data. This model contains no predictors and represents the simplest possible prediction approach. Comparing the RMSE of the fitted models to the null model allows us to determine whether the predictors meaningfully improve prediction accuracy.

mean_y <- mean(train_data$Y)

null_pred <- rep(mean_y, nrow(train_data))

rmse_null <- yardstick::rmse_vec(
  truth = train_data$Y,
  estimate = null_pred
)

rmse_null

[1] 948.3526


The null model produces an RMSE of about 948, meaning predictions based only on the mean outcome perform poorly. This indicates that models including predictors such as dose and participant characteristics provide much more accurate predictions than simply using the average exposure value for all individuals.

title: Model Fitting Exercise
text:
Cross-Validation
Although RMSE calculated on the training data provides an estimate of model fit, it may underestimate error because the models were evaluated on the same data used for fitting. To better assess how well the models generalize to new data, 10-fold cross-validation is performed. In this procedure, the training data are divided into ten subsets (folds). The model is repeatedly trained on nine folds and evaluated on the remaining fold, and this process is repeated until each fold has served as the validation set.

set.seed(rngseed)

cv_folds <- vfold_cv(train_data, v = 10)


Fit Models with Cross-Validation

model1_cv <- fit_resamples(
  model1_spec,
  Y ~ DOSE,
  resamples = cv_folds,
  metrics = metric_set(rmse)
)

model2_cv <- fit_resamples(
  model2_spec,
  Y ~ DOSE + AGE + SEX + WT + HT,
  resamples = cv_folds,
  metrics = metric_set(rmse)
)



Cross-Validation Results

collect_metrics(model1_cv)

# A tibble: 1 × 6
  .metric .estimator  mean     n std_err .config        
  <chr>   <chr>      <dbl> <int>   <dbl> <chr>          
1 rmse    standard    691.    10    67.5 pre0_mod0_post0

collect_metrics(model2_cv)

# A tibble: 1 × 6
  .metric .estimator  mean     n std_err .config        
  <chr>   <chr>      <dbl> <int>   <dbl> <chr>          
1 rmse    standard    646.    10    64.8 pre0_mod0_post0


The cross-validation results provide an estimate of model prediction error on unseen, new data. The dose-only model has a cross-validated RMSE of approximately 691, while the full model including additional predictors has a lower RMSE of about 646. These results are consistent with the training RMSE values and suggest that the full model provides better predictive performance while maintaining reasonable generalizability.


Changing the Random Seed

rngseed <- 4321
set.seed(rngseed)

cv_folds2 <- vfold_cv(train_data, v = 10)

This section added by Aylla von Ermland
Creating predictions for all 3 models (training data):
model1_fit (DOSE only) model2_fit (all predictors) mean_y (null model)

# Model 1 predictions (DOSE only)
pred_m1 <- predict(model1_fit, train_data) %>%
  bind_cols(train_data %>% select(Y)) %>%
  mutate(model = "DOSE only")

# Model 2 predictions (all predictors)
pred_m2 <- predict(model2_fit, train_data) %>%
  bind_cols(train_data %>% select(Y)) %>%
  mutate(model = "All predictors")

# Null model predictions
pred_null <- train_data %>%
  mutate(
    .pred = mean_y,
    model = "Null model"
  ) %>%
  select(Y, .pred, model)

Combine into one dataset and plotting observed vs predicted:

pred_all <- bind_rows(pred_m1, pred_m2, pred_null)

ggplot(pred_all, aes(x = Y, y = .pred, color = model)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 5000), ylim = c(0, 5000)) +
  labs(
    title = "Observed vs predicted values",
    x = "Observed (Y)",
    y = "Predicted",
    color = "Model"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()








The null model produces a horizontal line because all predictions are equal to the mean outcome. The dose-only model produces three horizontal bands corresponding to the discrete dose levels, indicating that predictions depend only on dose. The model including all predictors performs best, with points more closely aligned to the 45-degree line, although substantial variability remains. Notably, the model tends to underpredict higher observed values, suggesting potential model misspecification or nonlinear relationships.
Model predictions and uncertainty:

set.seed(rngseed)

boot_data <- bootstraps(train_data, times = 100)


library(purrr)

# Generate predictions for model 2 on training data
pred_model2 <- predict(model2_fit, train_data) %>%
  bind_cols(train_data)

# Compute residuals
residual_df <- pred_model2 %>%
  mutate(residual = .pred - Y)

# Plot residuals vs predicted
ggplot(residual_df, aes(x = .pred, y = residual)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, color = "black") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-2500, 2500)) +
  labs(
    title = "Residuals vs predicted Values (model 2)",
    x = "Predicted values",
    y = "Residuals"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()








The residuals are pretty spread out, so the model isn’t super precise. The model isn’t fully capturing the pattern in the data and might be missing something or be too simple.

library(rsample)

set.seed(rngseed)

# Create bootstrap samples
boot_obj <- bootstraps(train_data, times = 100)

# Pre-allocate matrix
pred_bs <- matrix(NA, nrow = 100, ncol = nrow(train_data))

# Loop through bootstrap samples
for (i in seq_len(100)) {
  
  boot_sample <- analysis(boot_obj$splits[[i]])
  
  # Fit model using lm (safe + numeric output)
  fit_boot <- lm(Y ~ DOSE + AGE + SEX + WT + HT, data = boot_sample)
  
  # Store predictions 
  pred_bs[i, ] <- predict(fit_boot, newdata = train_data)
}

Compute uncertainty:

# Quantiles: 89% CI + median
pred_summary <- apply(pred_bs, 2, quantile, probs = c(0.055, 0.5, 0.945))

pred_summary <- t(pred_summary)
pred_summary <- as.data.frame(pred_summary)

colnames(pred_summary) <- c("lower", "median", "upper")

Combine with original predictors:

# Original predictions (tidymodels → extract .pred)
orig_pred <- predict(model2_fit, train_data)$.pred

plot_df <- train_data %>%
  mutate(
    pred_point = orig_pred,
    lower = pred_summary$lower,
    median = pred_summary$median,
    upper = pred_summary$upper
  )

Plot for uncertainty visualization:

ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = Y)) +
  geom_point(aes(y = pred_point), color = "black", alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_point(aes(y = median), color = "black", alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper), color = "black", alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 5000), ylim = c(0, 5000)) +
  labs(
    title = "Observed vs predicted with Bootstrap",
    x = "Observed values",
    y = "Predicted values"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()








The model does okay overall since the points follow the diagonal, but there’s still a lot of scatter so it’s not super accurate. The error bars get bigger at higher values, so it’s less reliable there. You can also see it tends to underpredict the bigger values since a lot of points fall below the line.
